sections.text: Welcome back to AP Computer Science A. Module Four — iteration. This is the lesson where your code stops being calculator math and starts doing real work. Loops are how you put a computer to work on your behalf.

Modern computers do billions of operations every second. Loops are how we hand them that scale. Without loops, every program is just one line, one calculation, one answer. With loops, three lines of code can process a million records. That's the unlock today.

Three things to cover. One — while loops, for unknown counts. Two — for loops and the enhanced for-each, for known counts and clean traversal. Three — the four classic loop patterns, plus the off-by-one and infinite-loop traps that bite every CS A student at some point.

While loop. You give it a condition. As long as that condition is true, it keeps running the body. The check happens before each iteration — so if the condition starts false, the body never runs at all. Use this when you don't know up front how many times you'll loop.

Concrete example. Count down from ten. Set n to ten. While n is greater than zero, print n, then decrement. The loop runs ten times — ten, nine, eight, all the way to one — then n hits zero, the condition fails, and the loop exits. Clean and simple.

For loop. Three parts inside the parentheses, separated by semicolons. Init runs once at the very start. Cond is checked before every iteration. Step runs after every iteration. The classic shape — for int i equals zero, i less than five, i plus plus — loops exactly five times. Use this when you know the count.

Famous problem. Sum the numbers from one to one hundred. Start sum at zero. For i from one to one hundred, add i to sum. After the loop, sum equals five thousand fifty. Gauss did this in his head as a child. You just did it in three lines of Java.

Enhanced for, also called for-each, added in Java five. For int x colon arr — read it as for each x in arr. Cleaner than indexing when you just want to visit every element. One catch — it's read-only. Assigning to x inside the loop does NOT change the array. Use this for traversal, not for mutation.
Your turn. Write a loop that sums the squares of one through ten. Hint — for i from one to ten, add i times i to a running total. Pause now, write the loop, press play when you're ready.

Time. Declare int total equals zero. For int i equals one, i less than or equal to ten, i plus plus, add i times i to total. After the loop, total equals three hundred eighty five. One plus four plus nine, all the way up to one hundred.

Loops inside loops. A nested loop. The outer loop walks the rows. For each row, the inner loop walks the columns. A three by three multiplication table needs two for loops — the outer one for r, the inner one for c — and inside, you print r times c. Same shape works for grids, matrices, any two-dimensional structure.

Four loop patterns to memorize. Sum — a running total. Count — a counter for matches. Max — track the largest seen so far. Min — track the smallest. Almost every loop on the AP exam is one of these four. Recognize the pattern, write the template, fill in the condition.

Off-by-one — the most common loop bug in the universe. If you write i less than or equal to arr dot length, you'll try to access arr at arr dot length, which does not exist. ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException. The fix is one character — use strictly less than, not less than or equal to. Indices go from zero to length minus one.

Infinite loop. You wrote a while loop, set n to ten, and forgot to decrement n inside the body. The condition never changes, the loop never exits, your terminal freezes. Kill it with Control-C. The rule — always make sure the loop variable moves toward the exit condition. If it doesn't, you've written an infinite loop.

Recap. While for unknown counts. For for known counts. Enhanced-for for read-only traversal. Nested loops for grids. The four patterns are sum, count, max, and min. Off-by-one is strictly less than, not less than or equal to. Infinite loops happen when the counter never changes. Your assignment is in the dashboard.

Reminder — this video is the overview. To actually master iteration, here's the rest. Read the Iteration chapter on Runestone. Do the Codingbat Logic-2 and Loops practice — twelve problems will lock in the patterns. Submit the assignment in your dashboard. Book a check-in with your advisor if any of the four patterns feel fuzzy. See you in Module Five — Writing Classes.
